title: My Last Duchess
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  FERRARA


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s my last Duchess painted on the wall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Looking as if she were alive. I call
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That piece a wonder, now: Frן¿½ Pandolfן¿½s hands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Worked busily a day, and there she stands.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Willן¿½t please you sit and look at her? I said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Frן¿½ Pandolfן¿½ by design, for never read
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strangers like you that pictured countenance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The depth and passion of its earnest glance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But to myself they turned (since none puts by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The curtain I have drawn for you, but I)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And seemed as they would ask me, if they durst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How such a glance came there; so, not the first
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are you to turn and ask thus. Sir, ן¿½twas not
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her husbandן¿½s presence only, called that spot
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of joy into the Duchessן¿½ cheek: perhaps
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Frן¿½ Pandolf chanced to say ן¿½Her mantle laps
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Over my ladyן¿½s wrist too much,ן¿½ or ן¿½Paint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Must never hope to reproduce the faint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Half-flush that dies along her throat;ן¿½ such stuff
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was courtesy, she thought, and cause enough
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For calling up that spot of joy. She had
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A heart . . . how shall I say? . . . too soon made glad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Too easily impressed; she liked whateן¿½er
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She looked on, and her looks went everywhere.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sir, ן¿½twas all one! My favour at her breast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dropping of the daylight in the West,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bough of cherries some officious fool
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Broke in the orchard for her, the white mule
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She rode with round the terrace, all and each
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would draw from her alike the approving speech,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or blush, at least. She thanked men, good; but thanked
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Somehow . . . I know not how . . . as if she ranked
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With anybodyן¿½s gift. Whoן¿½d stoop to blame
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This sort of trifling? Even had you skill
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In speech, (which I have not), to make your will
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quite clear to such an one, and say, ן¿½Just this
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or that in you disgusts me; here you miss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or there exceed the markן¿½, and if she let
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Herself be lessoned so, nor plainly set
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her wits to yours, forsooth, and made excuse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eן¿½en then would be some stooping; and I choose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never to stoop. Oh sir, she smiled, no doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wheneן¿½er I passed her; but who passed without
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Much the same smile? This grew; I gave commands;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then all smiles stopped together. There she stands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if alive. Willן¿½t please you rise? Weן¿½ll meet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The company below, then. I repeat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Count your masterן¿½s known munificence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is ample warrant that no just pretence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of mine for dowry will be disallowed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though his fair daughterן¿½s self, as I avowed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At starting, is my object. Nay, weן¿½ll go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Together down, sir. Notice Neptune, though,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Taming a sea-horse, thought a rarity,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which Claus of Innsbruck cast in bronze for me!
meaning:
"My Last Duchess" is a quintessential example of Robert Browning's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
